params.text:
The law behind your records

Almost every record you keep contains personal data — information that identifies a living person. In the UK this information is protected by the UK GDPR and the Data Protection Act 2018. These laws do not exist to make your job harder; they set out how personal data must be handled so the people you support keep control of information about their own lives.

The regulator for data protection is the Information Commissioner’s Office, known as the ICO. Your employer is responsible for following the law, but every entry you make is part of how your organisation meets it.

params.alt: Illustration for Data protection in depth

params.text:
The data protection principles

The UK GDPR sets out core principles for handling personal data. In your everyday recording they mean you should:

  • Lawfulness, fairness and transparency: have a proper reason to record, and be open about it.
  • Purpose limitation: use information only for the care purpose it was gathered for.
  • Data minimisation: record what is relevant and necessary, not everything you know.
  • Accuracy: keep records correct and up to date.
  • Storage limitation: keep records only as long as they are genuinely needed.
  • Integrity and confidentiality: keep the information secure at all times.

Health and care records also count as special category data, which needs extra protection because it is so sensitive.

params.question: Under the data protection principle of data minimisation, what should you record in a person’s care notes?

answers.text: Only the information that is relevant and necessary for their care.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Data minimisation means recording what is needed for the care purpose, not everything you happen to know.

answers.text: Everything you have learned about the person, in case it proves useful one day.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Recording more than is needed goes against data minimisation and can expose private details unnecessarily.

answers.text: As little as possible, even if some relevant care details are left out.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Records must still be complete enough for safe care; minimisation is about relevance, not leaving gaps.

answers.text: Whatever the person’s family would like to see included.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. What you record is guided by the care purpose and the law, not by what others would prefer to add.
